title: ANT/ENT Endings, Adverbs, and the J-Sound
description: Convert -ant/-ent words to Spanish by adding -e. Form adverbs with -mente. The English J-sound becomes a throat sound in Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: What was the word for 'is' in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es. Good. Words ending in -ant or -ent often become Spanish by just adding -e. So 'important' becomes importante.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is important'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es importante. Good. How would you say 'it's not important'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No es importante. Good. How would you say 'different'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Diferente. Good. How would you say 'it is different'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es diferente. Good. How would you say 'constant'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Constante. Good. If you add -mente to a word, you get the '-ly' equivalent. So 'constantly' is constantemente.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'constantly'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Constantemente. Good. How would you say 'legally'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Legalmente. Perfect. How would you say 'illegally'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ilegalmente. Good. The word 'real' in Spanish is real — same word, just stress the end. How would you say 'really'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Realmente. Good. Fun fact: real also means 'royal' in Spanish. Real Madrid is Royal Madrid.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'normally'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Normalmente. Good. You can use normalmente like 'usually'. How would you say 'normally it's important'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Normalmente es importante. Good. When a word has a J-sound in English, that sound becomes a soft throat sound in Spanish — like the G in Argentina.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'general'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: General. Good. How would you say 'generally'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Generalmente. Good. How would you say 'originally'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Originalmente. Good. How would you say 'naturally it's original'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Naturalmente es original. Good. Naturalmente can also mean 'obviously'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: One more pattern: words ending in -ible or -able often work in Spanish too. 'Possible' becomes posible. How would you say 'probable'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Probable. Good. How would you say 'probably'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Probablemente. Good. How would you say 'possibly'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Posiblemente. Good. How would you say 'possibly it's original'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Posiblemente es original. Good. With these patterns, you can experiment freely — if you're close, people will understand you.

outro.text: You can now convert three types of English words into Spanish: words ending in -al, words ending in -ant or -ent, and words ending in -ible or -able. And you can turn any of them into adverbs with -mente.
Try making a sentence with words we haven't covered — maybe 'possibly it's not normal' or 'generally it's natural'. Say it out loud.
You're building real sentences now. Next time, we'll add more tools for making longer, more interesting ones.
